author: Augustine of Hippo
book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
Love the Root of All Good Things; Cupidity, of All Evil Ones.
title: Love the Root of All Good Things; Cupidity, of All Evil Ones.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: Chapter 21 [XX.]—Love the Root of All Good Things; Cupidity, of All Evil Ones.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: The “capacity,” then, of which we speak is not (as he supposes) the one identical root both of good things and evil.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: For the love which is the root of good things is quite different from the cupidity which is the root of evil things—as different, indeed, as virtue is from vice.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: But without doubt this “capacity” is capable of either root: because a man is not only able to possess love, whereby the tree becomes a good one; but he is likewise able to have cupidity, which makes the tree evil.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: This human cupidity, however, which is a vice, has for its author man, or man’s deceiver, but not man’s Creator.

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: It is indeed that “lust of the flesh, and the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life, which is not of the Father, but is of the world.”

verses.book: A Treatise on the Grace of Christ, and on Original Sin.
verses.text: And who can be ignorant of the usage of the Scripture, which under the designation of “the world” is accustomed to describe those who inhabit the world?
